content: I'm thinking about writing a simple fs in rust. It will be as simple as debugfs, only interacting with VFS and not touching real block devices. When it's done, we may extend it to touch real devices.
Sounds great! Related: https://gitlab.cs.washington.edu/sm237/bentofs (Samantha Miller will be joining Zulip)
Awesome!! The idea of Bentofs looks really cool to me!! Thank you!

How can we abstract bit flags??

e.g. file mode

mode = S_IRUSR|S_IRGRP|S_IROTH

Is it approriate to use const???

pub const S_IRUSR: u32 = 400;
pub const S_IROTH: u32 = 4;
...

@Gary Guo Hi, Gary, I think I need your help.

I came across a problem when abstracting tree_descr https://elixir.bootlin.com/linux/v5.12/source/include/linux/fs.h#L3299.
tree_descr defines a list of files and is used by functions like simple_fill_super.
for instance: https://elixir.bootlin.com/linux/v5.12/source/drivers/xen/xenfs/super.c#L49

The problem is in rust we implement file_operations as a trait.
So the struct will be like this:

pub struct TreeDesc<T: FileOpener> {
    name: CStr,
    ops: T,
    mode: i32,
}

each one of them has a different type.

Now how can we aggregate them in to a container(array, vec, list) and pass as a parameter??
I think it'll probably easier just to store a &'static bindings::file_operations

Gary Guo said:

I think it'll probably easier just to store a &'static bindings::file_operations

Thanks for replying, sorry for the delay. :)

But we have abstract file_operations as a trait to user, do you meant we expose bindings::file_operations as well??

Or maybe I can use a macro which converts types implementing file_operations trait into bindings::fille_operations, and insert them into a container. e.g.

pub struct ops1;
pub struct ops2;
pub struct ops3;

impl FIleOperations for ops1 {...}
impl FIleOperations for ops2 {...}
impl FIleOperations for ops3 {...}

let tree_desc = treedescr! {         // <------------- the macro will conver ops into bindings::file_operations and put them into a vector
      "file1", ops1, 777
      "file2",ops2,S_IRUSR|S_IROTH
      "file3",ops3, 644
}

But we have abstract file_operations as a trait to user, do you meant we expose bindings::file_operations as well??

Maybe construct a vtable using the mechanisms infile_operations.rs? Might require to make a few things pub(crate) or #[doc(hidden)] pub.

Gary Guo said:

But we have abstract file_operations as a trait to user, do you meant we expose bindings::file_operations as well??

Maybe construct a vtable using the mechanisms infile_operations.rs? Might require to make a few things pub(crate) or #[doc(hidden)] pub.

I am pretty satisfied with the macro solution. :grinning_face_with_smiling_eyes:
Look at my implementation:
https://github.com/foxhlchen/linux/blob/d7fe46d7416199e85682abf0144518600ffc52e6/samples/rust/rust_ramfs.rs#L77
https://github.com/foxhlchen/linux/blob/d7fe46d7416199e85682abf0144518600ffc52e6/rust/kernel/fs.rs#L243

We are currently wrapping kernel structures such as dentry or inode. Our initial implementation is a struct Dentry(bindings::dentry) with repr(transparent). We would just implement AsRef, Deref and *Mut counterparts, but this creates exclusive references to the kernel objects (wiith mem::transmute). We noticed that you already have File and FileRef, but these just hold a pointer. Is it unsound to create references like we do with DerefMut? Is there any way to wrap, say, an Inode so that you can access it's fields without getters and setters for every field?

FYI, I took different approach. I provided to_c_xx() function to expose raw pointer to the user. When you use this, you take full responsibilities. It's true we can't wrap everything and users inevitably need to interact with c inode structure. But in this way, you have a clear boundary.

Generally, we don't want to expose C data structures to non-kernel-crate.

The approach we've taken in the existing code, and gpio/irq/power/amba/pci code soon to come is similar to File, FileRef and Task where we wrap raw pointers and only expose functionality currently needed.

Part of the reason we don't want to expose raw pointer to C data is because it requires unsafe code to access, it has no information about lifetime (and for how long callee can hold on to pointers), it lacks information about which locks need to be acquired to access which fields, and there's a mismatch wrt memory model. IOW, exposing C data structure to Rust is a minefield that we think is better not exposed for now.

Okay, then we will mirror what Filedid :+1: One more question: To me, the File and FileRef relationship feels a bit turned-around. Wouldn't it make sense that FileRef wraps a pointer and then File would just be an "owning" FileRef (implement Drop and everything)? Or what is the benefit of having FileRef be implemented in terms of File?

Oh and I have another question :sweat_smile: : Having File as just a pointer eliminates the danger of having aliased mutable references. What if we want to go from a &mut File to one of its fields (for example f_path; don't know if we ever want to touch that field specifically), we would have something like fn path_mut(&mut self) -> &mut bindings::path. This could violate the invariants for &mut again, right?

Another question: We implemented an ExpectK trait to get output using pr_emerg before panicking with expectk for Result and Option. Do you want these additions in the kernel crate? What is otherwise your approach when facing e.g. an unexpected null pointer in the crate?

Eventually the bindings module will be private to kernel, so we never want to expose anything defined in it directly to users.

FileRef is a ManuallyDrop<File> without into_inner, it came years after File. I guess it would make sense to have FileRef implement everything and have File add the Drop implementation as you suggest. (If we do that, we should do the same for Task and TaskRef for consistency.)
I guess we should call pr_emerg in panic handler before calling BUG.

Hi all:

Currently, Isn't it possible to pass a custom context to chardev? The convert() method in FileOpenAdapter trait for chrdev::Registration returns &() simply, and it seems no API available for a user to pass a custom context across file operations like miscdev.

Have you checked FileOpener?? By implementing FileOpener for your struct RustFile,
You can create customized data. It then will be set as file's priva_data.

Fox Chen said:

Have you checked FileOpener?? By implementing FileOpener for your struct RustFile,
You can create customized data. It then will be set as file's priva_data.

Thanks for your reply.

I know FileOpener trait, and I can implement this trait for my struct as well. However, for a higher abstraction of chrdev (rust/kernel/chrdev.rs) , it has already implemented FileOpener trait for chrdev::Registration.

I picked this line below from samples/rust/rust_chrdev.rs:

chrdev_reg.as_mut().register::<RustFile>()?;

Will it be better if we add a context argument to register<T>() function?

let context = Context:new();
chrdev_reg.as_mut().register::<RustFile>(context)?;

miscdev provides a good example:

impl<T: Sync> Registration<T> {
    pub fn new_pinned<F: FileOpener<T>>(
        name: &'static CStr,
        minor: Option<i32>,
        context: T,
    ) -> Result<Pin<Box<Self>>> {
        let mut r = Pin::from(Box::try_new(Self::new(context))?);
        r.as_mut().register::<F>(name, minor)?;
        Ok(r)
    }
}
But where do you store it??
For miscdev when dev is being open file::priv_data will point at your struct miscdev. If that is true for chrdev, then you can do it.
That is the problem. I will try to find a way to solve it.

Hello guys, eventually I will project and implement a FS from scratch using the Rust API, But first I want to implement a simple FAT16 driver, for getting confortable with the FS subsystem Rust API.

Looking at the kernel docs and in the fs/fat implemenation, the mount function is done like that:

static struct dentry *vfat_mount(struct file_system_type *fs_type,
               int flags, const char *dev_name,
               void *data)
{
    return mount_bdev(fs_type, flags, dev_name, data, vfat_fill_super);
}

static struct file_system_type vfat_fs_type = {
    .owner      = THIS_MODULE,
    .name       = "vfat",
    .mount      = vfat_mount,
    .kill_sb    = kill_block_super,
    .fs_flags   = FS_REQUIRES_DEV | FS_ALLOW_IDMAP,
};

But, reading the rustdocs it was not clear to me how can I achieve that using the Rust API. Someone could give me some advice?

Thanks in Advance.

Hello, I want to implement an in-kernel filesystem for https://github.com/anuvu/puzzlefs. Looking at samples/rust/rust_fs.rs, it seems that rust filesystem support is still under development and there hasn't been any activity in the past half year. Another difficulty I see is including crates like serde and serde_cbor into my kernel module. I would appreciate any feedback and I would also like to get involved with the filesystem side of rust-for-linux in order to move towards my goal.

Another difficulty I see is including crates like serde and serde_cbor into my kernel module.

Currently we do not include third-party crates in the kernel, though there have been a few discussions about potentially supporting some in the future.
If you do an out-of-tree module, I guess you can include whatever code you want? I am not sure that the out-of-tree template has any cargo support though.
If you are referring to this one https://github.com/Rust-for-Linux/rust-out-of-tree-module then it doesn't seem to support dependencies on other crates
I think it is OK to integrate cargo support into that one, @Miguel Ojeda ?
I see no reason why out-of-tree modules should not be able to do whatever they want

Andreas Hindborg said:

If you do an out-of-tree module, I guess you can include whatever code you want? I am not sure that the out-of-tree template has any cargo support though.

Anybody can do, of course, whatever they want, as long as it complies with the license, but it does not mean it can be upstreamed.

Andreas Hindborg said:

I think it is OK to integrate cargo support into that one, Miguel Ojeda ?

No, because then people will end up relying on code that they may not realize they cannot upstream. If we did support that, then it would need to come with a huge banner. It is best to wait until we decide (or not) to support third-party crates in the kernel (and which ones, too).

Also please note I was talking about third-party crates, not about cargo support. In other words, what is likely (or rather, possible) to happen is that we include some third-party crates in the kernel tree, not that we allow any crate in general or that cargo support is added in general (which could still happen, but way less likely in the near-term).

I see. @Ariel Miculas so while not officially supported, you could take the out-of-tree template and add cargo support to that if you wanted. You could also just vendor all your dependencies. There is cargo-vendor for that. But be aware of licence requirements if you distribute he result. I have no idea how licencing works if you distribute modules as binary or source where the licence is not compatible with GPL2.
Of course if you want your contribution to eventually be included in the upstream kernel, this is not a good approach.

Thanks @Andreas Hindborg, I would rather choose an approach supported by the community. I'm still trying to figure out if there's enough rust support in kernel for implementing my filesystem driver or if I'm better off doing this in C (which is not ideal).
Are there any plans for extending the filesystem support?

Some people is interested in developing filesystems (or related tooling) in Rust, so I think it is likely that we will see progress there, but it depends on who has time to submit abstractions for it.
